title: CHAPTER 6 —
PREPARING PRESENTATIONS
segments.body_html:
A slide is not a document. This sounds obvious but almost nobody acts like it's true.

Chapter 6 — Preparing Presentations — A document is complete. You hand it to someone and leave the room, and they can understand it. It contains everything —

beats.text: A slide is not a document. This sounds obvious but almost nobody acts like it's true. A document is complete. You hand it to someone and leave the room, and they can understand it. It contains everything , context, argument, evidence, qualification. The words carry the whole weight of the communication.

segments.label: 01 / 12 · HOOK

title: THE UPSTREAM DECISION:
PURPOSE BEFORE SLIDES
segments.body_html:
—
Audience Goal — Audience Goal — first option applied to the chapter example.
Slides Included — Slides Included — first option applied to the chapter example.
Slides Excluded — Slides Excluded — first option applied to the chapter example.

beats.text: Most presentations go wrong before the software opens. The person sits down, opens PowerPoint or Google Slides, creates a title slide, and starts adding content. Each slide is a unit of information they know. The deck grows by accretion. By the end, they have twenty-two slides covering everything they could think to say about the topic.

segments.label: 02 / 12 · THE UPSTREAM DECISION: PURPOSE

title: CONSISTENCY IS
NOT AESTHETICS.
segments.body_html:
Once you know what your presentation is for, you open the software. And here the temptation is to start making things lo

Consistency Is Not Aesthetics — Resist this. Looking good is the wrong goal. Looking consistent is the right goal, and it is not the same thing.

beats.text: Once you know what your presentation is for, you open the software. And here the temptation is to start making things look good. Resist this. Looking good is the wrong goal. Looking consistent is the right goal, and it is not the same thing.

segments.label: 03 / 12 · CONSISTENCY IS NOT AESTHETICS

segments.section: TEXT AND IMAGE ARE DIFFERENT T
title: TEXT AND
IMAGE ARE DIFFERENT THINGS
segments.body_html:
A slide has two channels: text and image. The failure mode is using them to say the same thing. The opportunity is using

Text and Image Are Different Things — The wrong pattern: a chart showing quarterly revenue growth sits on the slide, and below it is a text label: "Quarterly

beats.text: A slide has two channels: text and image. The failure mode is using them to say the same thing. The opportunity is using them to say different things that together mean more than either alone. The wrong pattern: a chart showing quarterly revenue growth sits on the slide, and below it is a text label: "Quarterly revenue growth, 2022, 2024." The label says what the chart already shows. Anyone who reads the label does not need to look at the chart.

segments.label: 04 / 12 · TEXT AND IMAGE ARE DIFFERENT T

title: HEADLINES ARE ARGUMENTS,
NOT LABELS.
segments.body_html:
The most common failure in slide text is the label. "Q3 Revenue." "Competitive Landscape." "Implementation Timeline." Th

Headlines Are Arguments, Not Labels — A headline is an argument. It says something that could be true or false, that the evidence on the slide either supports

beats.text: The most common failure in slide text is the label. "Q3 Revenue." "Competitive Landscape." "Implementation Timeline." These are titles for filing cabinets, not claims about what the audience should think. A headline is an argument. It says something that could be true or false, that the evidence on the slide either supports or does not. "Q3 revenue grew 15 percent, but three regions declined" is a headline. It makes a claim. The chart on the slide confirms or refutes it.

segments.label: 05 / 12 · HEADLINES ARE ARGUMENTS, NOT L

title: THE ARCHITECTURE OF
THE WHOLE.
segments.body_html:
A presentation is a sequence, and sequences have structure. The structure is not arbitrary. It follows from the purpose.

The Architecture of the Whole — Most effective presentations follow a version of this shape: orient, complicate, resolve. You orient the audience — here

beats.text: A presentation is a sequence, and sequences have structure. The structure is not arbitrary. It follows from the purpose. Most effective presentations follow a version of this shape: orient, complicate, resolve. You orient the audience , here is the situation, here is why we are talking about this today. You complicate it , here is the problem, the gap, the thing that is not working. You resolve it , here is the solution, the plan, the ask.

segments.label: 06 / 12 · THE ARCHITECTURE OF THE WHOLE

title: THE DISCIPLINE
OF CUTTING.
segments.body_html:
Every presentation benefits from having half its slides removed.

The Discipline of Cutting — This is not hyperbole. The discipline of building a presentation is the discipline of cutting. You start with too much —

beats.text: Every presentation benefits from having half its slides removed. This is not hyperbole. The discipline of building a presentation is the discipline of cutting. You start with too much , too many slides, too many words per slide, too many visual elements per slide. The work is removing things until what remains is exactly what the audience needs and nothing more.

segments.label: 07 / 12 · THE DISCIPLINE OF CUTTING

title: EXERCISES
segments.body_html: 1.→2.→3.→4.→5.

beats.text: Take any presentation you have given or attended , find an actual slide deck, or recall one clearly. Identify one slide that is a document (complete, self-contained, dense with text) and one slide that is a cue (a single claim, a chart, a number). Write one sentence explaining what job each slide was trying to do and whether it succeeded. If the document-style slide had to be cut to cue-style, what is the one thing that must remain? Tests: the document vs.

segments.label: 08 / 12 · EXERCISES

·Ask it to walk through Bertin's seven visual variables one at a time, with a concrete example of when each is the right
Ask"Bertin's Sémiologie graphique was published in French in 1967 but not translated to English until 1983. What did the si
Add the framing"Answer as if you're a 1960s French cartographer explaining to a colleague why a particular world-map design has gone wr

beats.text: The ideas in this chapter didn't appear from nowhere. Jacques Bertin was working on Sémiologie graphique (1967) , the foundational theory of visual variables that every modern chart, dashboard, and presentation slide quietly inherits decades before most people had heard of visual communication in presentations. Here's a prompt to find out more , and then make it better.

segments.label: 09 / 12 · AI WAYBACK MACHINE

title: PROMPTS
segments.body_html:
Create a single-file D3 v7 HTML chart using a quantitative y-axis with a zero baseline, 4-6 labeled categories on the x-

Prompts — Create a single-file D3 v7 HTML chart using a quantitative y-axis with a zero baseline, 4-6 labeled categories on the x-

beats.text: Create a single-file D3 v7 HTML chart using a quantitative y-axis with a zero baseline, 4-6 labeled categories on the x-axis, direct value labels above marks, one primary red series and neutral supporting marks. The figure should represent: side-by-side of two slides on the same topic , left slide packed with full-sentence bullets (document style), right slide with a single claim headline and a chart (cue style) , caption: same information, opposite assumptions about what the audience will do with it.
